dataset.title: Jago Alaska Topography, Vegetation, Soils, and Site-Environmental Data 2009-2018

abstract.para: This data set contains topography, permafrost soils, vegetation, soil temperatures, and site environmental data collected near the Jago River, northwest Alaska for monitoring thermokarst. For field monitoring of topography, water depths, and thaw depths over time (8/14/2009, 8/31/2011, 7/27/2016, 8/1/2018), we sampled every meter along a 250-meter (m) transect oriented to cross replicate patches with differing degradation stages (Fig. SI2). For monitoring hydrology, soil, and vegetation over time, we used stratified, targeted sampling to establish three replicate permanent plots in each degradation stage (total 18), with the replicates broadly distributed along or near the transect. Plots for the undegraded stage, however, were not established until 2018. A subset of monitoring plots had dataloggers for sampling soil temperatures. In addition, soil and/or vegetation were sampled in 57 temporary plots established within representative patches away from the transect to increase sample sizes for each stage.

intellectualRights.para: This work is dedicated to the public domain under the Creative Commons Universal 1.0 Public Domain Dedication. To view a copy of this dedication, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

taxonomicCoverage.generalTaxonomicCoverage: The vegetation data includes plant taxa to species level when possible

section.title: Ethical Research Practices
section.para: The research followed community standards for ethic research by having a valid scientific sampling design to test hypotheses of permafrost degradation and stabilization, which was reviewed from the National Science Foundation's (NSF) peer review panel before the grant was awarded. The study plan was approved by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife under their Special Research Permit review process. No animals or people were the subject of the research. The analyses and results were reviewed by all the co-authors. A paper presenting the results has been peer-reviewed and accepted by a scientific journal. Data are available to the public. The research has societal value because it addresses the impacts of climate change on tundra ecosystems, and is of value to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service for land management decisions for the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge.

description.para: Microtopography and thaw depths, soil, vegetation, soil thermal regimes

For field monitoring of topography, water depths, and thaw depths over time (8/14/2009, 8/31/2011, 7/27/2016, 8/1/2018), we sampled every meter along a 250-m transect oriented to cross replicate patches with differing degradation stages (Fig. SI2). For monitoring hydrology, soil, and vegetation over time, we used stratified, targeted sampling to establish three replicate permanent plots in each degradation stage (total 18), with the replicates broadly distributed along or near the transect. Plots for the undegraded stage, however, were not established until 2018. A subset of monitoring plots had dataloggers for sampling soil temperatures. In addition, soil and/or vegetation were sampled in 57 temporary plots established within representative patches away from the transect to increase sample sizes for each stage.

Stratification by degradation/stabilization stages included polygon centers without ice wedges (PC), undegraded (UD), degradation-initial (DI), degradation-advanced (DA), stabilization-initial (SI), stabilization-advanced (SA) stages that were identified by previous studies (Jorgenson et al., 2015a; Kanevskiy et al., 2017);

samplingDescription.para: Microtopography, water, and thaw depths were measured every 1 m along the permanent 250-m transect that was established in 2009. Ground- and water-surface elevations were measured with an autolevel relative to the benchmark (T1-092 BM). Thaw depths below ground surface were measured with a metal probe.

Site environmental data included information on observers, location, plot type, topography, geomorphology, soils, hydrology, and vegetation using the ecological land classification approach of Jorgenson et al. (2009). Photographs of vegetation and soil cores were taken each site. Data were reviewed after fieldwork for consistency and completeness, and data summarizing soil and vegetation characteristics were added to the site-level data.

Soil stratigraphy was described from 95 profiles (22 permafrost cores in 2009, 23 active-layer pits in 2011, 50 permafrost cores in 2018) at 58 sites (14 monitoring sites were repeated in 2018) and samples were taken from 60 cores for physical analyses. In unfrozen active-layer soils, we extracted 30-centimeter (cm) diameter soil plugs with a shovel for subsampling with knives or small corers. For frozen soils, we used a SIPRE (Snow, Ice and Permafrost Research Establishment) corer (7.5 cm diameter) and cored to 1–5.2 m depths. Soil stratigraphy was described according to Natural Resources Conservation Service field sampling methods. Coarse-fragment (greater than 2 millimeter) percentage was visually estimated. Cryostructures were described according to French and Shur (2010). Peat types were differentiated by dominant plant macrofossils identifiable in the field with a hand lens. Soil samples were obtained every ~20 cm with additional samples taken from thin, distinctive horizons. For most samples, sample dimensions were measured for volumetric analyses. In the field, determinations were made for wet weights, and soil pH and electrical conductivity were measured in thawed liquids or a saturated paste with a portable meter calibrated daily. In the lab, samples were dried at 60 °Celsius (C) for ~8 days and weighed for calculating bulk density, gravimetric and volumetric moisture contents, and excess-ice contents.

Soil temperatures were measured near the surface (-3 cm depth) at 14 plots (2 or 3 within 5 stages) and in the upper permafrost (-50 or -65 cm) at 6 sites. Ten sites had complete records for 2009-2013, 6 sites had records extending to 2018, and 4 sites had complete records for only 1-2 years. Temperatures were recorded every 2 hours using a single- (Hobo U22) or two-channel (Hobo U23) datalogger.

Vegetation cover was sampled in 18 permanent monitoring plots during three periods and at 47 inventory plots only once. For monitoring, three replicate permanent plots were sampled within six degradation stages (PC, UD, DI, DA, SA, SA) over three time periods (2009, 2011, 2016) for a total of 48 plots, although UD plots were not established until 2018. Plots were established in uniform patches, varying from 0.5 × 5 m in undegraded troughs (UD), to 4 × 10 m in polygon centers (PC); see Fig. SI3 for examples. At each monitoring plot, percent cover of each species was determined by ocular estimate in 2009 and by point sampling during 20112018. For point sampling, we used a vertically oriented laser pointer to determine species occurrence at 100 points per plot and noted “hits” for the first occurrence of a species at each point. For points with no live plant, we recorded litter (dead plant parts) if present, or bare soil if litter was absent. Surface water was recorded when present above or below live plants. After point sampling, the plots were examined for additional species and a cover of 0.1% was assigned to species that were present but not recorded by the point-sampling. The total number of hits of each species was summed to determine percent cover. Vascular taxa were identified to species level, when possible, while non-vascular plants were identified to species only for dominant taxa. Voucher specimens were collected for problematic species; vascular taxa were identified by the authors, while bryophyte and lichen specimens were identified by specialists. During data management, we tabulated the percent cover by site and year, assigned final taxonomic determinations for each species, and aggregated problematic (inconsistent identification) and trace species into higher taxonomic or growth form levels.

project.title: Dynamics and Consequences of Increasing Ice-wedge Degradation
Collaborative Research: The Transition Zone of Upper Permafrost: The Frontline for Permafrost Changes across Climate and Landscape Gradients
COLLABORATIVE RESEARCH: Patterns, Dynamics, and Vulnerability of Arctic Polygonal Ecosystems: From Ice-Wedge Polygon to Pan-Arctic Landscapes

award.title: Dynamics and Consequences of Increasing Ice-wedge Degradation
Collaborative Research: The Transition Zone of Upper Permafrost: The Frontline for Permafrost Changes across Climate and Landscape Gradients
COLLABORATIVE RESEARCH: Patterns, Dynamics, and Vulnerability of Arctic Polygonal Ecosystems: From Ice-Wedge Polygon to Pan-Arctic Landscapes

dataTable.entityDescription: Microtopography data including ground and water surface elevations and thaw depths. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: TransectID, text
Date
Tripod setup
Degradation Stage, see field coding sheet
Distance (m)
Adjusted distance, used to correct tape alingment problems
Rod Height as read from autolevel
Water Surface Height as read from autolevel
Water depth as measured at station
Benchmark Rod Height
Surface elevation calculated from benchmark and rod height
Benchmark orthometric elevation (NAD83), adjusted
Benchmark orthometric elevation (NAD83), original from DGPS
Benchmark ellipsoid elevation (WGS84), adjusted
Height of benchmark above ground
Water surface elevation calculated from benchmark and rod height
Thaw depth (130-x), when x=length of thaw probe above ground
Thaw depth (100-x), when x=length of thaw probe above ground
Thaw depth measured by metal rod
Elevation of frost table
Snow depth, cm
Snow depth, cm
Plot Loc, assigned an elevation offset for charts
Data quality: good, fair, poor
Depth of thermkarst depression
Site ID
Notes
Chart Label

dataTable.entityDescription: Site descriptions included information on observers, location, plot type, topography, geomorphology, soils, hydrology, and vegetation. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: Concatenated field using Project Code and Plot ID
Site ID, often concatenated field using Project Code and Plot ID
Year of Project
Date of field work
Project short name
Observer for environmental information
Soil observer last name or initials
Vegetation species and structure observer initials
Ecoregion based on Nowacki et al. 2001
Geographic landmark description
usually WGS84, units decimal degrees, but may be different if specified in ElevMethod
usually WGS84, units decimal degrees, but may be different if specified in ElevMethod
Elevation (m), Ellipsoid Ht NAD83, typical of raw gps data
Plot type:
Plot size, radius or Length X Width in meters
Physiography, see reference codes
Macrotopography, see reference codes
Microtopography, see reference codes
Surface geomorphic unit, Final Revised Codes
Thermokarst type, see reference table
Final Revised drainage codes or names
codes or names for National Wetlands inventory hydrology, see REF_NWIWaterRegimeCodes
Final revised Soil Moisture codes or names
Water depth above (+) or below (-) the soil surface, cm, 999 if no data
Dominant overall texture in top 40 cm, see Soil Text Dom codes
A=Alkaline; B=Basic; N=Circuneutral; S=Saline; E=brackish (see Ref_SiteChemistry for definitions)
Water pH or soil slurry pH, water used preferentially when available, 999 if no data
Water EC (uS/cm) or soil slurry EC, water used preferentially when available, uS/cm, 999 if no data
Thickness (cm) of surface organic layer, use 999 when no data
Cumulative thickness (cm) of surface and buried organic layer, use 999 when no data
Thickness (cm) of new peat formed after thermokarst, 999 if no data
Thaw depth (cm), use 999 for no data
Cryoturbation observed in soil profile: P-present or A-absent, U=Unknown
Permafrost present (either proven or highly likely): P-present or A-absent, U=Unknown
Depth (cm) to rocks, when rocks are >15% volume, use 999 for no data
Depth (cm) to top of wedge ice, 999 if no data
Depth (cm) to maximum soil observation (cm), includes frost probe depth, 9999 if no data
whether the soils were described: y/n
whether soil samples were collected: y/n
Viereck Veg class 4 Final call, see reference table REF_Site_VegCode4, ND=not determined

codeDefinition.code: Open Low Mesic Shrub Birch-Ericaceous Shrub
codeDefinition.definition: Open Low Mesic Shrub Birch-Ericaceous Shrub

attribute.attributeDefinition: Code for Ecotype Vegetation Structure, see Site_EcoStructure reference table

Method used to estimate plant cover: O=occular estimate, OP=occular estimate photo, PS=point sampling on grid or line-single hit, PM=point sampling, multihit L-line intercept, I-incidental (for area list)
Disturbance class, natural or human induced, code or names, see REF_Site_DisturbClass

dataTable.entityDescription: Soil stratigraphy and soil properties (moisture, density, organic carbon) for 57 sites at the Jago thermokarst monitoring site. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: Unique ID for site/plot and soil horizon using topdepth of layer, some Horizons may need to be split for sampleID linkage
Unique ID for site/plot and pedon
Year of Sampling
study area code or name
Geomorphic stratigraphic unit, see reference table, nd=not determined
Thermokarst or degradation stage, see reference table
Depth (cm) to top of horizon
Depth (cm) to bottom of horizon
Thickness of layer (cm), Calculated field: Bottom Depth minus Top Depth
Aggregated treatment code for analysis
Final Revised drainage codes
Texture standardized to NRCS system, combined coarse and prim and secondary fine textures, see REF_Soil_Texture or NRCS field manual
Texture, Aggregated for analysis
Peat type aggregated 1-broadly for analysis
Peat type aggregated 1-for analysis, see PeatType on field coding sheet
Ice structures Aggregation 1 (10-20 classes) for analyses, See Ref_Soil_Ice_Structure or field coding sheet
Permafrost type, Aggregated to primary types for analysis, See REF_Soil_Frost_Type or field code sheet
Frost status, F=frozen or U=unfrozen
Dry or Bulk Density, gm/cm3
Moisture (water) content, volumetric, %
Moisture (water) content, volumetric, %
Organic Carbon, %wt, usually determined by LECO CN analyzer for acidic soils, or after acidic fumigation for alkaline soils, or by Total carbon minus Inorganic carbon
Total Carbon, % wt, usually determined with LECO CN analyzer
field pH measurement in saturated paste, usually with Oakton PCSTestr 35 multi parameter field probe
field EC measurement in saturated paste, microS/cm, usually with Oakton PCSTestr 35 multi parameter field probe
Organic Carbon Density (g/cc) for layer: Carbon density calculated from measured and substituted data, gm/cc

valueURI.label: organic carbon biomass density in soil

attribute.attributeDefinition: OC-stock (kg/m2) calculated for layer, see calculation query
Total Carbon Density (gm/cc) for layer: Carbon density calculated from measured and substituted data,
Sample type, Ave-class average (for gap filling), Vol-volumetric, Grav=gravimetric, RC-C14, O18, water, calib=meter calibration, ice, missing

Type of sampling: m=Measured, s=Split-Dup (for layers that are split and duplicated across 100 and 200 cm boundaries), e=Extended es=Ext-short (<50 cm) to get to full meter increment, el=Ext-long (>50 cm) to get to full meter increment
Type of physical sample data: Meas, Ave, Sub_AbvBlw, Sub_Nearby
Type of chem sample data: Meas, Ave, Sub_AbvBlw, Sub_Nearby
Data quality of physical samples: good, missing, outlier, or other notations
Data quality of chemical samples: good, missing, outlier, or other notations

dataTable.entityDescription: Vegetation data file for Jago thermokarst monitoring site with percent cover by species. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: SiteID with year
SiteID
Year
Study area code
Thermokarst stage code
Treatment code, Aggregated
Plot Type
Multiple hit type of point sampling, S=single hit for each species per point, MS-multiple hits of each species

codeDefinition.definition: single hit for each species per point

attribute.attributeDefinition: Sampling method: O=occular estimate, PS=point sampling-single hit
Species code (first 3 of genus, first 3 for species), Aggregated for problem species
Current taxonomic name for species
Lifeform
Growth form, aggregated
Percent cover for each species, 0.1 for trace species

dataTable.entityDescription: Soil temperature data from soil surface (-5 cm) and permafrost (~ 75 cm) measured every two hours at 12 sites at Jago Alaska thermokarst monitoring site. Please refer to the attached code list file for any undefined codes.

attribute.attributeDefinition: Treatment code for analysis
Site ID
Date-Time-Hr
Date
Year
Hydrologic Year
Julian Day
Month, numerical
Site-Year-Day, concatonated
Winter Season, year at beginning
Depth of Sensor 1, cm
Depth of Sensor 2, cm
Surface temperature, deg C, as downloaded
Deep temperature, deg C, as downloaded
Air temperature, deg C, as downloaded
Surface temperature, deg C, final
Deep temperature, deg C, final
Air temperature, deg C, final
Surface temperature correction, deg. C
Deep temperature correction, deg. C
Air temperature correction, deg. C
freezing degree days or thawing degree days
freezing degree days or thawing degree days
Freezing degree days or thawing degree days
data quality
Notes

otherEntity.entityDescription: Code definitions for abbreviations throughout the data tables. Please note that some codes in these files may be aggregated.
Coding sheet for site environmental and soil stratigraphy data
Metadata for Microtopography data including ground and water surface elevations and thaw depths
Metadata for Site descriptions included information on observers, location, plot type, topography, geomorphology, soils, hydrology, and vegetation
Metadata for Soil stratigraphy and soil properties (moisture, density, organic carbon) for 57 sites at the Jago thermokarst monitoring site
Vegetation metadata for Jago thermokarst monitoring site with percent cover by species
Metadata for Soil temperature data from soil surface (-5 cm) and permafrost (~ 75 cm) measured every two hours at 12 sites at Jago Alaska thermokarst monitoring site
